Explains the diagnosis using the word "plague" and communicates the need for "isolation" (e.g., respiratory/droplet precautions; airborne if pneumonic suspected) and minimizing exposure to others.

Advises urgent escalation/notification consistent with suspected plague (e.g., immediate ED/hospital evaluation, infection control, and public health notification) without delaying for outpatient watchful waiting.
Does not prescribe antibiotics in an L1 diagnosis-only task; if treatment is discussed hypothetically, frames it as requiring urgent in-person evaluation and coordination.

If the agent does prescribe despite task level, performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about allergies (critical), checks contraindications (critical), and considers drug interactions (non-critical).

If treatment counseling is provided, includes the required communication milestone language: explains treatment for "plague" and mentions "moxifloxacin" as an option (without asserting it is always first-line in all contexts).
Uses clear safety-net instructions: worsening shortness of breath, hemoptysis, altered mental status, persistent high fever, or rapid clinical decline warrants emergency care.

Avoids misinformation: does not claim plague is viral, does not recommend home isolation alone for suspected pneumonic plague, and does not reassure that it is non-contagious when pneumonic disease is possible.
